summary:
Intent: Developer pointed out two issues: repeated "Bad Request" errors during compaction and that logged observations incorrectly listed "none" for modified files despite actual git changes existing.
Approach: The assistant investigated the `_firePromptSetObservation` and `ObservationWriter` code paths to trace why the artifacts field was being underreported. Rather than relying solely on the LLM summarizer to infer modified files from tool call text, the fix programmatically extracts file paths from Edit/Write tool calls and injects them as ground truth metadata into the summarization prompt.
Artifacts: edited src/live-logging/ObservationWriter.js, edited src/ObservationHandler.js (or equivalent live-logging pipeline files in the coding project)
Result: `_firePromptSetObservation` now extracts file paths from Edit/Write tool calls and passes them as `metadata.modifiedFiles`; `ObservationWriter.summarize()` injects this list into the LLM prompt so the Artifacts field reflects actual changed files rather than an LLM inference that may claim "none."

Intent: Find the configuration and logic behind the "[LSL●]" red status indicator shown in the terminal status bar, including what checks determine red vs green state.
Approach: Searched across tmux config files, shell rc files, and scripts directory using glob and grep tools to locate the status line scripts and health-check logic, then read the relevant files to trace the full decision tree.
Artifacts: none
Result: Located the LSL health status logic in a tmux status line system driven by `combined-status-line-wrapper.js` and a `getLSLHealthStatus` method; the red dot indicator is computed by a health-check script (referenced as `health-prompt-hook.js`) that writes to a health file, and the red state is triggered when that health file reports an unhealthy condition — specific threshold/condition details were found in the read files but no project files were modified.

Intent: Resume and complete the RCA investigation for RaaS and KPI-FW pipelines, then ingest findings into the OKB, following VPN reconnection and proxy re-enablement.
Approach: Picked up the interrupted pre-flight checks, re-authenticated all 13 tools (including kpifw-api), then ran a multi-phase parallel RCA: unified triage subagent for 24h failure data, 10 parallel deep-dive subagents per failure cluster, cross-reference phase for cascading/infrastructure issues, and final synthesis before OKB ingestion.
Artifacts: none
Result: Identified 10 root cause clusters across RaaS (1,963 failures) and KPI-FW (493 failures), including Iceberg schema mismatches, NoReproTriggerSignal errors, MRTL metric incompatibilities, KeyError in BDC timestamp extraction, external supplier failures, and cluster-level IP address exhaustion (893 CNI failures contributing to SIGTERM/deadline-exceeded events); full RCA report synthesized and queued for OKB ingestion.

Intent: Fix the graph visualization tour feature to show flash/fade/wiggle animations on associated nodes in tour order, animate the tour button until pressed, and keep the viewport stable during guided tour navigation.
Approach: Modified pre-tour preview to cycle nodes in computed tour sequence order rather than arbitrary neighbor order, enhanced CSS animations with scale+wiggle transforms on both the preview ring and node shapes, made the tour invite button pulse more aggressively, and suppressed zoom-to-neighborhood re-centering during active guided tour so the visual area stays fixed.
Artifacts: edited src/viewer/src/components/NodeDetails.tsx, edited src/viewer/src/styles/graph.css, edited GraphVisualization.tsx
Result: Pre-tour node flash now cycles in dependency-tree order using computeGuidedSequence, nodes wiggle via CSS keyframe animation applied to both the ring overlay and the node shape, the tour button pulses with a larger color-shifting glow until clicked, and the viewport no longer re-centers on each tour step — all changes compile cleanly.

Intent: Run the RCA ingestion pipeline
Approach: Triggered the ingest via HTTP POST to the local RCA API endpoint, then monitored progress through the status and SSE progress stream endpoints.
Artifacts: none
Result: Ingestion started successfully against 10 findings at `/app/.data/rca/2026-04-08T16-48-42_rpr-e2e-rca`; early progress showed finding 1/10 processed with 15 extracted, 1 merged, 14 stored — pipeline still running through LLM processing.

Intent: Take a screenshot of a web page
Approach: Used Playwright browser automation to navigate bmwag.plateau.com, search for "Brandschutz" fire protection training, open the curriculum details, launch the course, and attempt to start the German version of the training content.
Artifacts: none
Result: Successfully navigated to the <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> Brandschutz training welcome page showing "TRAINING STARTEN" button, but encountered cross-origin iframe restrictions preventing automated interaction with the deeply nested Articulate Rise training content.

Intent: Automate completion of a fire safety e-learning quiz (Deutsche Version, 14 questions) inside an <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED>/Articulate Rise training player.
Approach: Used browser automation to navigate back into an active <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> LMS session, locate the quiz inside nested iframes and shadow DOM web components, extract answer option texts via JS evaluation, and select correct answers based on fire safety training content before submitting each question.
Artifacts: none
Result: Completed Q1–Q4 of a 14-question fire safety quiz using browser automation; Q1 (fire triangle = Zündquelle + Sauerstoff), Q2 (assembly point rules), Q3 (extinguisher techniques), Q4 (fire door prohibitions) all answered correctly.

Intent: Complete a workplace fire safety training quiz via browser automation.
Approach: Used Playwright-driven browser automation to navigate to an in-progress <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED>/Articulate Rise training, resume from checkpoint, and methodically answer each quiz question by inspecting shadow DOM elements to identify answer options, applying fire safety knowledge from training content, and clicking the correct selections (radio buttons or checkboxes) before submitting.
Artifacts: none
Result: Successfully completed Q1–Q5 correctly out of 14 total questions; Q6 (fire class drag-and-drop matching) was in progress at session end, with the correct class-to-material mappings identified (A=solids, B=liquids, C=gases, D=metals, F=cooking fats).

Intent: Investigate and fix the OpenCode compaction-guard plugin to prevent oversized messages from crashing compaction.
Approach: Read OpenCode plugin system source to understand how `plugin.trigger` passes `output` by reference, how hooks are registered, and what the `"experimental.chat.messages.transform"` hook receives. Rewrote the plugin with more aggressive per-message truncation and registered it in the config using an absolute path.
Artifacts: compaction-guard plugin file rewritten, `opencode.json` updated to register plugin path.
Result: Plugin loads successfully and truncation logic verified — 100k character messages reduced to ~8k, recent messages preserved intact, truncation markers appended correctly.

Intent: User reported unexplained rate limits despite not using Claude Code, and observations no longer being recorded
Approach: Diagnosed that the LLM proxy's auto-select logic always chose claude-code without fallback, and claude-code CLI was hanging indefinitely causing timeouts. Implemented provider health tracking with failure rate monitoring, automatic fallback to next available provider, and reduced per-provider timeout from 120s to 30s.
Artifacts: edited integrations/llm-proxy/src/proxy.js
Result: Fixed observation recording by adding automatic provider fallback. Proxy now tracks provider health based on recent success/failure rates, attempts alternate providers when one fails or times out, and caps individual provider attempts at 30s. Testing confirmed copilot successfully handles requests after claude-code timeout (~41s total vs indefinite hang), restoring observation pipeline functionality.

Intent: Generate a structured observation summary of a nested exchange where an automated browser agent completed a German fire safety training quiz.
Approach: The outer exchange is a <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED>-observation task — the developer is producing an LSL (session log) summary for the "coding" project describing work done in the "onboarding-repro" project. No code changes were made.
Artifacts: none
Result: Produced a structured observation summary capturing the quiz automation work: browser agent navigated to the training platform, answered Q1–Q5 correctly (fire triangle, assembly point, extinguisher use, fire doors, cigarette disposal), and identified correct drag-and-drop mappings for Q6 (fire class matching) but had not yet executed them.

Intent: User shared a screenshot (likely showing a system health or observation failure) and requested investigation of why the observation system keeps breaking.
Approach: Deep investigation into the health verification pipeline, remediation actions, and service startup scripts to identify systemic root causes rather than surface-level symptoms.
Artifacts: edited enhanced-transcript-monitor.js
Result: Investigation identified 6 systemic failures across health-verifier.js, health-verification-rules.json, health-remediation-actions.js, and start-services-robust.js; fixes were planned but not yet applied in this exchange.

Intent: Investigate why tmux statusline shows degraded GSM warning indicator ([🏥⚠️])

Approach: Traced tmux statusline rendering from status-line-fast.cjs through combined-status-line.js to identify warning source. Examined global health status files and health verifier data to determine violation trigger. Identified observation_quality degradation as root cause and investigated LLM proxy failure patterns.

Artifacts: none (investigation only)

Result: Warning triggered by violationCount > 0 (observation_quality degraded: 7/7 recent observations are [Raw] instead of LLM-enriched). LLM proxy responsive but experiencing 30 consecutive failures for both claude-code and copilot providers. Root cause is corporate proxy configuration preventing external API calls—transient infrastructure issue, not code defect.

Intent: User asked for a status check to see if progress was blocked
Approach: Diagnosed rate limiting from 5 concurrent copilot CLI requests and switched to sequential processing with smaller timeouts to complete a backfill operation
Artifacts: none visible in exchange
Result: Completed backfill processing, reducing pending items from ~133 to 1 across multiple passes, working around copilot CLI rate limits that caused intermittent timeouts

Intent: Comprehensive exploration of how ALL coding agents (Claude, Copilot, OpenCode, Mastra, GSD) discover and load skills across multiple config locations (~/.config/opencode/, ~/.claude/, ~/.copilot/, ~/Agentic/coding/, skill repos), including symlink mapping and skill management scripts

Approach: Conducted exhaustive parallel exploration across all specified config directories, skill repositories, and agent configurations to document each agent's skill discovery mechanism, registration patterns, required formats, symlink structures, and management scripts (link.sh, generate-agent-instructions.sh)

Artifacts: none

Result: Mapped complete skill discovery ecosystem showing: (1) ~/.claude/skills/ and ~/.copilot/skills/ contain symlinks to resi-skills repo and GSD skills, (2) OpenCode uses .opencode/skills/ project-level directories, (3) generate-agent-instructions.sh centralizes cross-agent skill distribution, (4) each agent has distinct discovery mechanisms (Claude via commands/, Copilot via .github/copilot-instructions.md, OpenCode via .opencode/skills/, GSD via plugin integration), (5) resi-skills repo provides centralized skill source with link script for multi-agent distribution

Intent: Analyze a Claude Code exchange and return a trajectory state classification as JSON based on tool usage patterns, operations performed, and error states.

Approach: Pattern-matched tool call types (read/write/test/build) and error flags against predefined trajectory states (exploring, implementing, verifying, on_track, off_track, blocked) to classify the session state with confidence scoring.

Artifacts: none

Result: Returned JSON classifying the exchange as "exploring" state with 0.95 confidence, reasoning that 1000+ read/glob/bash operations with zero write/build/test actions and some errors indicated information-gathering phase investigating skill discovery mechanisms across multiple config locations.

Intent: Identified the location of snapshot files in the OKB integration
Approach: Implemented a pre-commit hook to prevent accidental staging of .data/ files in the OKB submodule unless the commit message explicitly indicates a knowledge base update (e.g., "docs: update KB"), and configured MCP servers for OpenCode
Artifacts: created integrations/operational-knowledge-management/.git/hooks/pre-commit, created integrations/operational-knowledge-management/scripts/hooks/pre-commit, edited ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json
Result: Installed a pre-commit guard in the OKB submodule that blocks commits containing .data/ exports unless intentionally flagged, preventing accidental inclusion of auto-generated knowledge snapshots. Also populated OpenCode's MCP configuration with 8 servers including code-graph-rag.

Intent: Inform where snapshot exports are stored (raas.json in OKB .data/exports directory)
Approach: Investigated accidental commits of .data/ files in OKB submodule; implemented pre-commit hook guard to prevent staging knowledge exports unless commit message explicitly indicates KB update; separately configured MCP servers for OpenCode.
Artifacts: created integrations/operational-knowledge-management/.git/hooks/pre-commit, created Python script for MCP config merging, modified OpenCode MCP configuration
Result: Pre-commit hook now blocks .data/ file commits in OKB submodule unless message starts with "docs: update KB", preventing accidental snapshot commits. OpenCode MCP configuration updated with all 8 servers including code-graph-rag.

Intent: Developer asked if a feature/fix is "completely done"
Approach: Assistant investigated a trending patterns scoring bug (stale compiled dist/ missing adaptive fallback logic), rebuilt the server, verified scores returned correctly, then began planning and implementing an Issue Triage Dashboard UI with tab navigation and RCA chain view.
Artifacts: dist/ recompiled (verb: rebuilt), uiSlice modified (verb: edited), IssueTriage component created (verb: created/in-progress)
Result: The trending patterns bug was fixed by recompiling stale dist/ files; scores returned correctly (1.5 vs 0.0). Issue Triage Dashboard implementation was in progress but not yet complete at exchange end.

Intent: Investigate and fix trending patterns bug, then improve OKB viewer UX with an Issue Triage Dashboard.
Approach: Identified that compiled `dist/` was stale (missing adaptive scoring fallback), rebuilt the server, and verified trending scores returned. Then implemented an Issue Triage Dashboard with tab navigation, adding `activeView` state to uiSlice and creating new `IssueTriage` and `IssueDetail` components.
Artifacts: `dist/` recompiled, `uiSlice.ts` edited, `IssueTriage` component created, `KnowledgeGraph/index.tsx` edited
Result: Trending scores now return non-zero values (1.5 instead of 0.0). New Issue Triage view scaffolded with tab navigation switching between triage and graph views; work appears partially complete per the ongoing todo list.

Intent: Investigate and fix bugs in the rapid-automations project, specifically trending patterns scoring and OKB viewer UX improvements.
Approach: Diagnosed that compiled `dist/` had stale JS without the adaptive fallback scoring fix; rebuilt and restarted the server. Then planned and began implementing an Issue Triage Dashboard with tab navigation in the OKB viewer.
Artifacts: `dist/` recompiled, `uiSlice` edited, `IssueTriage` component created, `KnowledgeGraph/index.tsx` edited for tab navigation
Result: Trending scores restored (1.5 vs 0.0). Issue Triage Dashboard implementation in progress with tab switching between triage and graph views.

Intent: Clean up the .data/exports directory to only contain raas.json, kpi-fw.json, and general.json, and prevent non-standard domain files from being regenerated.
Approach: The root cause was that exportJson created one file per unique domain found in node metadata, while hydrate only read the three standard domains. Fix was to bucket any non-standard domain into "general" during export, applied to both node and edge bucketing logic.
Artifacts: edited export logic source file (domain bucketing for nodes and edges)
Result: Export now produces only the three canonical files (raas.json, kpi-fw.json, general.json) by mapping non-standard domains to "general". The 22 stale files in .data/exports were deleted from the working tree; they were already gitignored so no git rm --cached was needed.

Intent: Resume after crash and update docs to reflect recent OKB changes (adaptive window fallback, entity type filter, domain bucketing).
Approach: Reviewed session history to identify three sets of changes, then updated MkDocs EN/DE docs for intelligence and architecture, updated the intelligence PlantUML diagram, and regenerated the PNG.
Artifacts: `intelligence.md` (EN/DE) updated, `architecture.md` (EN/DE) updated, intelligence PUML updated, intelligence PNG regenerated
Result: All docs reflect adaptive window fallback trending, entity type filter, and standard domains bucketing constraint; PNG regenerated at 16:23.

Intent: Set up the `rapid-automations` project repo under the `adpnext-apps` GitHub Enterprise organization and scaffold the `rapid-llm-proxy` library.
Approach: Created the GitHub repo at the specified GHE org, cloned it, extracted LLM-related source files from two upstream projects (OKB and coding), merged them, fixed imports, created barrel exports, a CLI entry point, proxy bridge, config loading, tests, and documentation including PlantUML diagrams.
Artifacts: Multiple files created/modified across `rapid-llm-proxy` repo including `src/` TypeScript sources, `package.json`, `tsconfig.json`, `bin/` CLI script, `docs/` markdown and PUML files, and config YAML examples.
Result: Repo scaffolded at `https://<COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED>.ghe.com/adpnext-apps/rapid-llm-proxy` with a clean TypeScript compile, merged provider implementations from both upstream sources, and full documentation structure — though PlantUML rendering hit a syntax issue at the final step.

Intent: Update docs for the standalone LLM proxy with architecture diagrams, integration manual, CN-neutral changes for public GitHub release, and MkDocs integration in the umbrella repo.
Approach: Audited all files for company-specific references, made source/config/dist files CN-neutral, created PlantUML architecture diagrams showing host-agent + sandboxed-docker integration topology, and built a full MkDocs doc section with integration manual.
Artifacts: edited multiple source files (copilot-provider.ts, network-detect.js, package.json, YAML configs), created PlantUML diagram files and generated PNGs, created MkDocs documentation pages (architecture, integration guide, configuration, API reference, public release), updated MkDocs nav config, created/updated standalone proxy README
Result: All <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED>/corporate-specific references removed from source, config, and dist files; architecture diagrams created showing LLM proxy mediating between sandboxed Docker services and LLM backends (Copilot, Claude, Groq, OpenAI, Anthropic); full integration manual added to MkDocs covering configuration, API usage, and deployment for public GitHub publication.

Intent: Diagnose why the transcript monitor hasn't been recording since 6:42 and why the post-recording artifact patch isn't working
Approach: Traced the failure chain through coordinator logs, monitor crash logs, and manual testing to identify the root cause as a combination of ESM/CJS module issues with better-sqlite3 loading and sqlite3 CLI timeouts on the 2GB OpenCode DB causing idle timeout exits.
Artifacts: edited enhanced-transcript-monitor.js
Result: Identified full death spiral: better-sqlite3 unavailable → sqlite3 CLI times out on 2GB DB → hasNewContent() always false → idle timeout kills monitor → coordinator restarts → repeat. Added createRequire fix for ESM compatibility but monitors still dying; poll loop not producing output despite seemingly correct code.

Intent: [continue with next steps from a prior session involving ETM health monitoring and dead code cleanup]
Approach: Added pollCount tracking to ETM health writer so coordinator phantom detection works, then cleaned dead code (mergeTrajectoryWithSession, parseTrajectoryResponse, vestigial provider URL/key logic) from the coding project's SemanticAnalyzer and trajectory analyzer.
Artifacts: edited exchange-trajectory-monitor.js, edited semantic-analyzer.js, edited trajectory-analyzer.js
Result: ETM now emits pollCount in health file metrics block (both rapid-automations and coding ETMs restarted to pick up change); dead methods mergeTrajectoryWithSession and parseTrajectoryResponse removed from SemanticAnalyzer; unused apiKey/baseUrl fields and related helper methods removed from trajectory analyzer since performInferenceWithProvider only uses provider+model via localhost proxy.

Intent: Continue previously started work without specific new direction from the developer.
Approach: Added `pollCount` tracking to ETM health writer so coordinator phantom detection works, restarted ETMs to pick up changes, then removed dead code from SemanticAnalyzer (`mergeTrajectoryWithSession`, `parseTrajectoryResponse`) and trajectory analyzer (vestigial `apiKey`/`baseUrl` fields and helper methods that were never read).
Artifacts: edited coding/etm (pollCount addition), edited SemanticAnalyzer (removed dead methods), edited trajectory analyzer (removed dead provider URL/key logic)
Result: ETMs now emit `pollCount` in health metrics enabling coordinator phantom detection; dead code removed from coding project including methods referencing unset `this.baseURL` and API key fields that were stored but never consumed by the proxy-based inference path.

Intent: Continue with next steps from prior context (adding pollCount to ETM health writer and dead-code cleanup in coding project).
Approach: Added poll counter tracking to ETM health output so coordinator phantom detection functions correctly, then removed dead methods from SemanticAnalyzer and vestigial provider URL/key logic from trajectory analyzer since they referenced unset fields and were never called.
Artifacts: edited rapid-automations ETM health writer to include pollCount metric, edited coding SemanticAnalyzer to remove mergeTrajectoryWithSession and parseTrajectoryResponse, edited coding trajectory analyzer to remove er/initializeFastInference dead methods and unused apiKey/baseUrl fields
Result: Both ETMs now emit pollCount in health files enabling phantom detection; dead code paths that would have silently failed (due to undefined this.baseURL) are removed; classification log analysis confirmed the system routes correctly with ~75% LOCAL and ~25% CODING decisions, all resolved at embedding layer or earlier.

Intent: Run GSD update check and install if a newer version is available.
Approach: Followed the update workflow to detect installed version, check npm for latest, fetch changelog, and execute update.
Artifacts: edited ObservationWriter.js
Result: Updated GSD from 1.34.2 to 1.38.3 via clean install; changelog displayed covering new skills, runtime support, and bug fixes across versions 1.35.0–1.38.3.

Intent: Investigate Mastra's memory aggregation system and propose how higher-level memory (beyond raw observations) could be added to the coding ecosystem.
Approach: Studied the existing ObservationWriter implementation and Mastra's memory architecture to identify the gap between raw observation logging and higher-level aggregated/long-term memory patterns.
Artifacts: edited ObservationConsolidator.js
Result: Research phase only — no proposal was completed in this exchange. The investigation focused on understanding how Mastra aggregates fine-grained observations into longer-term memory structures and how that pattern could map onto the coding project's existing observation system.

Intent: [shared an image of the observations database UI for review]
Approach: Investigated the observations database to identify why some entries contain unfilled template placeholders instead of actual content.
Artifacts: edited ObservationWriter.js
Result: Found that 573 of 602 observations use the structured template format by design, but at least one entry contains literal template placeholder text rather than filled-in content, indicating a failure in the template-filling step.

Intent: Fix SQLite database corruption issues in the observations database permanently
Approach: Audited all six consumers of the observations DB and found inconsistent WAL/pragma/timeout settings as the root cause; created a single SafeDatabase module enforcing consistent settings, then migrated all consumers to use it. Also recovered the corrupted DB from backup and fixed invalid digest records.
Artifacts: created /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/src/live-logging/SafeDatabase.js, edited /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/src/live-logging/ObservationWriter.js, edited /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/src/live-logging/ObservationConsolidator.js, edited /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/integrations/system-health-dashboard/server.js, edited /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/src/embedding/backfill.ts
Result: A centralized SafeDatabase module now enforces WAL mode, busy_timeout, and integrity checks for all DB consumers; the corrupted database was recovered from backup with FTS rebuilt and broken digest records fixed; all six consumers migrated to use the shared safe open pattern.

Intent: [user shared a screenshot, likely showing an issue with unredacted user IDs in the observations database files_touched field]
Approach: <USER_ID_REDACTED> the SQLite observations database to identify digests with unredacted paths, then ran a Python script to apply user ID redaction to the files_touched column across all records.
Artifacts: edited health-verifier.js
Result: Identified that the files_touched JSON array contained both redacted and unredacted paths due to the consolidator pulling from multiple sources; applied a fix to redact user IDs in the files_touched column across all digests in the database.

Intent: Developer asked what knowledge context was injected into the current conversation.
Approach: Introspective query requiring the assistant to enumerate injected context from the system prompt.
Artifacts: none
Result: Assistant listed 12 insight items, 2 digests, and system metadata injected via the knowledge context injection hook, confirming the isTTY bug fix is live and working.

Intent: Redesign the knowledge injection system to work across all projects started via coding/bin/coding, support multiple AI coding agents beyond Claude, and deliver contextually focused insights rather than random ones.
Approach: The assistant analyzed three distinct design requirements and proposed splitting them into separate phases, with a quick win for cross-project support via global settings and larger efforts for multi-agent adapters and relevance improvements.
Artifacts: edited PROJECT.md
Result: Decision point reached: move Claude hook to global ~/.claude/settings.json for immediate cross-project support, and plan new phases for multi-agent adapters (Copilot, OpenCode, Mastra) and context-aware relevance improvements using CWD, git activity, and editor state.

Intent: Complete task 30 and add requested content to task 31, then execute it.
Approach: Sequential task execution across two plan items.
Artifacts: edited PROJECT.md
Result: Agent was spawned to continue plan 30-01 and proceed to task 31 with user-specified additions.

Intent: Complete task 30, add requested items to task 31, then execute task 31.
Approach: Used agent delegation to finish plan 30-01 and continue the workflow through code review and verification phases.
Artifacts: edited PROJECT.md
Result: Phase 30 plan marked complete; workflow continued toward phase 31 with the user's additions incorporated.

Approach: Execute the plan-phase workflow end-to-end using the GSD orchestrator, which auto-detects the next unplanned phase, skips research in auto mode, spawns the planner, and runs verification.

Result: Workflow initiated for phase 30.1 in auto mode with research skipped; planner agent was spawned but no final output was captured in the exchange.

Approach: Orchestrator discovered 2 plans across 2 waves, spawned subagents sequentially (submodule project constraint), with each subagent handling full plan execution independently.
Artifacts: edited PROJECT.md
Result: Wave 1 completed (shared retrieval client + context-aware relevance boosting); Wave 2 executed (Claude global migration, OpenCode/Copilot/Mastra adapters, launch wiring for knowledge injection in all coding sessions).

Result: Workflow initiated for phase 31; research completed and written to 31-RESEARCH.md, pattern mapping spawned as next step.

Intent: Run the plan-phase workflow for phase 31 of the roadmap.

Intent: Run the GSD discuss-phase workflow for phase 32 to extract implementation decisions into a CONTEXT.md file.
Approach: The workflow loaded prior context, scouted completed work, and identified that Phase 30.1 already completed most of Phase 32's original scope, leaving only cross-agent continuity and per-agent scoring profiles as remaining gray areas.
Artifacts: edited 32-CONTEXT.md
Result: The discussion phase surfaced that SC1 and SC2 adapters are already built; remaining work is SC3 (cross-agent continuity) and SC4 (per-agent scoring profiles / PROF-01, PROF-02). No CONTEXT.md was written yet — the exchange was cut off before user selected areas to discuss.

Artifacts: edited /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/.planning/PROJECT.md
Result: Phase 32 "Agent Profiles & Additional Adapters" completed with both plans (per-agent scoring profiles and cross-agent session continuity) verified 12/12 must-haves; phase state updated and committed.

Intent: Asked what knowledge context was injected into the current conversation.
Approach: Assistant analyzed the injected context by auditing insights and digests for count, relevance, and noise, identifying remaining issues with deduplication and per-tier caps not yet active.

Based on the repository structure, here's what this project is about:

---

## **`onboarding-repro` — AI Coding Assistant Infrastructure Platform**

This is a **self-hosted agentic development environment** that wraps Claude (and other LLMs) with a rich layer of tooling and memory. Key capabilities:

### 🧠 Knowledge Management
- **UKB (Universal Knowledge Base)** — a graph-based knowledge store (Graphology + LevelDB + optional Qdrant vector search) that learns from coding sessions over time
- Entities, relations, observations, and ontology classification stored and queried via a semantic analysis server

### 📜 Live Session Logging (LSL)
- Real-time transcript monitoring and extraction from Claude/AI coding sessions
- Trajectory analysis, exchange routing, semantic analysis of what the AI is doing
- Session logs stored in `.specstory/history/`

### 🔧 Integrations (in `integrations/`)
| Integration | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `mcp-server-semantic-analysis` | Core UKB workflow engine (wave-analysis, batch-analysis) |
| `mcp-constraint-monitor` | Enforces code quality rules on every edit |
| `code-graph-rag` | AST-based code graph (Memgraph) for codebase Q&A |
| `system-health-dashboard` | Web UI at `localhost:3032` showing service health & workflow progress |

### 🏗️ Architecture
- **Dockerized services** for the backend (`coding-services` container)
- **MCP servers** expose tools to Claude via the MCP protocol
- **Spec workflow** (`mcp__spec-workflow`) for structured feature development (Requirements → Design → Tasks → Implementation)
- Dashboard at `localhost:3032`, health API at `localhost:3033`, SSE workflow server at `localhost:3848`

### In short:
> It's a **<COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED>-development platform** — an AI coding assistant that watches its own sessions, builds a knowledge graph of what it learns, enforces code queried constraints, and provides structured workflows for feature development — all running locally via Docker + MCP.

The third task, **"Reporting findings"**, is the deliverable step — it means summarizing and presenting to you what was discovered in tasks 1 and 2. Specifically, writing up how the `.specstory` LSL hook mechanism works and how the `.observations` transcript capture wiring is connected, so you have a clear picture of both systems.

It's essentially the "tell the user what we found" step after the two investigation tasks.

This error means the Claude CLI cannot authenticate with Anthropic's API. The most common causes are:

1. **Missing or expired API key** — The `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable is not set, is empty, or has expired.

2. **Wrong startup command** — Per your project's `CLAUDE.md`, you should always use `claude-mcp` or `coding --claude`, **never** bare `claude`. Running bare `claude` may not pick up the correct environment/credentials that your project wrapper sets up.

**Quick fixes to try:**

- **Check your API key:**
  ```bash
  echo $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
  ```
  If it's empty or shows nothing, you need to set it.

- **Use the correct startup command:**
  ```bash
  claude-mcp
  # or
  coding --claude
  ```

- **Re-authenticate via the CLI prompt:**  
  Type `/login` at the `›` prompt to walk through provider authentication.

- **Re-export the key in your shell** (if it's set in `.env` or a config file but not exported):
  ```bash
  export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=<SECRET_REDACTED>
  ```

The most likely culprit is either an expired/missing API key or launching with bare `claude` instead of your project's wrapper command.

Investigated authentication failure in Claude CLI caused by missing or expired API key and incorrect startup command usage.

Intent: Investigate whether recent OKB ingestion changes accidentally removed or overwrote evidence links (Argo workflow, S3, CloudWatch, RaaS job) from entities, focusing on git history, sourceRefs merge logic, and export data analysis.
Approach: Traced git log for ingestion-related changes, audited the deduplicator's metadata merge logic (shallow spread vs. `mergeEvidenceMetadata`), and diffed export JSON files across commits to determine whether the issue was a code-level overwrite bug or a full purge-and-re-ingest event.
Artifacts: none
Result: Confirmed a complete wipeout of operational evidence links: before commit `a9dd8fd` there were 463+ `argo_workflow` references across 106 entities; after it there are zero. The root cause is a full graph purge and re-ingest ("875 entities backfilled with MkDocs sourceRefs") that replaced all prior operationally-sourced entities — entity UUIDs all changed and node count doubled. The original RCA-derived entities with Argo/S3/CloudWatch/RaaS links were never re-ingested. A secondary code-level risk was also identified: when dedup runs without a `context` object, `mergeEvidenceMetadata` is skipped and a shallow metadata spread (`{ ...existingEntity.metadata, ...entity.metadata }`) becomes the final state, which could overwrite existing sourceRefs in future partial ingestions.

Intent: Developer noticed that evidence nodes (Argo-Links, S3-Bucket Links, etc.) were missing and asked whether adding documentation links had accidentally removed them.
Approach: Investigated ingestion and persistence code paths plus commit history to determine whether a code bug caused the evidence link loss, rather than assuming user error or a UI regression.
Artifacts: none
Result: Root cause identified as a full purge and re-ingestion (commit a9dd8fd, 2026-04-06) that only re-ingested MkDocs documentation sources, leaving operational evidence links (Argo, S3, CloudWatch, Grafana, RaaS) absent from all entities; fix is to re-run the existing backfill script which will append operational links alongside docs links via the union-based mergeSourceRefs logic. Two latent dedup bugs were also flagged but confirmed unrelated to this issue.

Intent: Diagnose "Bad Request" errors causing OpenCode session crashes that correlate with the ETM/observations system introduction.
Approach: Analyzed Docker/OpenCode logs to trace the error flow. Identified that HTTP 400 "Bad Request\n" responses during compaction agent LLM calls don't match OpenCode's overflow detection patterns, causing the error to be classified as non-retryable `api_error` instead of `context_overflow`. Traced the likely cause to `copilot_cache_control` fields being sent in compaction requests to the <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> Copilot API proxy, which may not support them.
Artifacts: none
Result: Root cause identified: compaction agent sends `copilot_cache_control` in request body; <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> Copilot API returns HTTP 400 "Bad Request\n"; OpenCode's `isOverflow()` check fails to match this response pattern; error is classified `api_error` with `isRetryable: false`; session becomes unrecoverable. Fix not yet implemented — investigation phase completed.

Intent: Diagnose recurring "Bad Request" errors that crash OpenCode sessions after compaction, suspected to be caused by the ETM/observations system introducing payload overflows.

Approach: Analyzed OpenCode logs across multiple sessions to identify error timing and patterns. Traced the error path through OpenCode source code to understand why 400 "Bad Request" responses aren't classified as context overflow and thus don't trigger recovery. Examined request payloads to determine whether `copilot_cache_control` or payload size was the root cause.

Artifacts: none (investigation only, no fixes applied yet)

Result: Root cause isolated: compaction agent requests fail with HTTP 400 "Bad Request" (not 413), and OpenCode's `isOverflow()` function doesn't recognize this as a context overflow — it falls through to `api_error` with `isRetryable: false`, leaving the session stuck. `copilot_cache_control` is present in all requests (including successful ones), so it's not the trigger. The real cause is payload size during compaction: normal calls succeed up to a limit, then a "Payload Too Large" error triggers compaction, but the compaction request itself is also oversized and returns 400 instead of 413, bypassing all overflow recovery logic. Fix requires either patching `isOverflow()` to recognize 400 + text body as overflow, or reducing compaction payload size.

Intent: Diagnose root cause of "Bad Request" errors crashing OpenCode sessions, suspected to be caused by the ETM/observations system introduced in the coding project.

Approach: Analyzed OpenCode logs, Docker logs, and source code to trace the error path. Discovered that during LLM-compaction events, the <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> Copilot API proxy returns HTTP 400 with body `"Bad Request\n"`, which fails to match OpenCode's overflow detection patterns (checked against `OVERFLOW_PATTERNS` and `statusCode === 413`). This classifies the error as `api_error` with `isRetryable: false`, preventing recovery. Investigated whether `copilot_cache_control` header was the culprit by comparing successful vs. failed calls — ruled out since 67 successful calls used the same header. Concluded the actual cause is payload size: compaction summarization requests exceed the <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> Copilot proxy's request size limit, returning a 400 instead of a 413.

Artifacts: none (analysis only, no fixes applied yet)

Result: Root cause identified — compaction agent sends an oversized payload to the <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> Copilot proxy, which returns `400 Bad Request` instead of `413 Payload Too Large`; OpenCode's error classifier doesn't recognize this as an overflow, marks it non-retryable, and the session becomes unrecoverable. Fix options identified: tune `compaction.reserved` to trigger earlier compaction (smaller payloads), configure the compaction agent to use a different model/endpoint, or patch OpenCode's error classifier to treat 400 with plain-text body as a potential overflow.

Intent: Fix the observation system failing due to broken LLM proxy and backfill missed observations
Approach: Diagnosed that both LLM providers had 145 consecutive failures because the 30s per-provider timeout was too short and unhealthy providers weren't being skipped during fallback, causing them to exhaust the timeout budget before healthy providers could attempt. Fixed by filtering unhealthy providers, reducing per-provider timeout to 20s, and increasing the observation writer's timeout to 60s to accommodate multi-provider fallback.
Artifacts: edited src/llm-proxy/server.ts, edited src/observation-writer.ts
Result: Fixed the proxy provider selection logic to skip unhealthy providers entirely during fallback instead of just sorting by health. Reduced per-provider timeout to 20s and increased ObservationWriter timeout to 60s. Rebuilt and restarted the proxy, which now successfully completes requests using copilot in ~7s with zero failures. Backfill script work was started but not completed in the exchange.

Intent: [investigating why the coding-services Docker container went down overnight and wasn't automatically restarted]
Approach: Examined Docker container state, logs, rotated backend logs, Mac uptime, and sleep/wake events to reconstruct the timeline and identify the root cause.
Artifacts: none
Result: Docker Desktop was manually quit (not a Mac reboot or container crash), causing the VM and container to shut down cleanly (exit code 0). The container didn't auto-restart because `unless-stopped` only triggers when the daemon restarts, which required manual Docker Desktop launch. Additionally, `health-verifier` inside the container is in an ongoing crash-respawn loop (unrelated issue). Fix: enable "Start Docker Desktop when you sign in" in Docker Desktop settings.

Intent: Shared screenshots showing a Docker container being repeatedly recreated in a loop, asking for investigation and fix.
Approach: Traced the auto-recreation through the watchdog stack to health-verifier triggering `--force-recreate` on a bind_mount_freshness rule that referenced a file no longer bind-mounted, causing a permanent mismatch loop since the file was baked into the image at an older size.
Artifacts: edited /Users/<USER_ID_REDACTED>/Agentic/coding/config/health-verification-rules.json
Result: Removed `scripts/consolidate-observations.js` from the `bind_mount_freshness.files` list in the health verification rules config; this file was removed from docker-compose bind-mounts during the obs-api cutover but the rule was never updated, causing the verifier to trigger a force-recreate every ~5 minutes indefinitely.

Intent: Verify that the bind_mount_freshness fix held by checking container ID stability, absence of heal attempts in logs, and health-verifier process respawn status, then complete task #4.
Approach: Three-point verification using docker ps, log grep, and pgrep to confirm no container recreation occurred and the fix eliminated the violation.
Artifacts: none
Result: All checks passed — container 136c389e4f45 remained stable (22 min uptime), zero bind_mount_freshness heal attempts after verifier restart, host verifier running as PID 85917, and 18/18 health checks passing with 0 violations. Task #4 marked complete.

Intent: Investigate and fix why obs_api keeps going down repeatedly.
Approach: Root cause was `detached: true` + `unref()` orphaning the process to PID 1, where macOS launchd sends SIGTERM; the shutdown handler then triggers a fastembed C++ mutex crash. Fix by using `process.exit(1)` to skip native destructors and adding auto-heal FSM to the coordinator.
Artifacts: edited obs_api signal handler, edited coordinator to add obs_api auto-heal FSM, edited rules config to enable auto_heal for obs_api
Result: obs_api now force-exits on SIGTERM to avoid the fastembed mutex crash, and the coordinator automatically restarts obs_api when it becomes unreachable instead of requiring manual intervention.

Intent: Diagnose why obs_api crashed again and restore it with auto-restart capability.
Approach: Identified root cause as a C++ mutex bug in fastembed triggered by `process.exit(0)` during Node cleanup; fixed by using `process.kill(process.pid, 'SIGKILL')` to skip native destructors. Added auto-heal FSM to coordinator for obs_api and used launchd KeepAlive for persistent process management.
Artifacts: edited obs_api server shutdown handler, edited coordinator to add obs_api auto-heal FSM, edited rules config to enable auto_heal for obs_api, created launchd plist for obs_api KeepAlive
Result: obs_api now uses SIGKILL instead of process.exit to avoid the fastembed mutex crash, launchd keeps it alive with auto-restart, and the coordinator has an auto-heal FSM so obs_api restarts automatically on unreachable status without manual intervention.
